title: High fidelity preservation of photonic hyperentanglement in a free-space optical delay line
authors: Yu Guo, Arya Chowdhury, Pranay Tiwari, Jia Boon Chin, Anindya Banerji, Alexander Ling
abstract: Photonic hyperentanglement enables increased information capacity and enhanced functionality for quantum communication and networking. However, synchronization of hyperentangled photon pairs requires maintaining correlations simultaneously across multiple degrees of freedom (DOFs). The preservation of polarization and energy-time entanglement in hyperentangled photon pairs is demonstrated using a free-space optical delay line based on nested Herriott cells. After a delay of 647 ns, a two-photon interference visibility of 93.9(3)% is observed in the energy-time DOF, while a CHSH parameter of 2.758(5) is obtained in the polarization DOF. These results confirm that entanglement correlations in both DOFs are preserved after propagation through the delay line. They demonstrate that free-space optical delay lines are compatible with complex photonic quantum states and provide a promising route toward delay-based memories for synchronization and multiplexing in quantum networks.
summary: This paper demonstrates that a free-space optical delay line can preserve hyperentangled photon states in both polarization and energy-time degrees of freedom for up to 647 ns. By using an all-reflective nested Herriott cell, the researchers achieved high-fidelity entanglement preservation, suggesting a scalable route for synchronization and multiplexing in quantum networks.
main_problem: The challenge of maintaining simultaneous correlations across multiple degrees of freedom (hyperentanglement) in photon pairs for synchronization in quantum networks.
main_result: Demonstrated high-fidelity preservation of both polarization and energy-time entanglement after a 647 ns delay using a free-space optical delay line.
method: The researchers used a nested Herriott cell for the delay and employed an unbalanced Michelson interferometer for energy-time analysis and quantum state tomography for polarization characterization.
model_or_system: An experimental platform consisting of a type-0 PPKTP crystal pumped by a 405.2 nm laser to generate hyperentangled photon pairs at 780.0 nm and 842.6 nm, passed through an all-reflective nested Herriott cell delay line.
key_observables: Energy-time visibility, CHSH parameter (S), polarization state fidelity, and two-photon interference fringes.
important_parameters: 647 ns temporal delay, 93.9% energy-time visibility, CHSH parameter of 2.758, and a 30 THz bandwidth.
main_assumptions: The energy-time visibility is partially limited by the intrinsic contrast of the analysis interferometer.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup for SPDC, the delay line, and analyzers; Fig 2 displays coincidence histograms and interference fringes; Fig 3 presents polarization correlation fringes and reconstructed density matrices.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of hyperentanglement synchronization, describes the experimental setup (SPDC source and Herriott cell), details the measurement techniques for different degrees of freedom, presents the results for entanglement preservation, and concludes with implications for quantum networks.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networking as it provides a room-temperature, broadband alternative to cryogenic quantum memories for managing multi-DOF quantum states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Experimental setup for generation, propagation, and characterization of hyperentangled photon pairs in a free-space opti- cal delay line (FSODL). (a) Hyperentangled photon pairs are generated via spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) in a PPKTP crystal. The signal photon propagates through the FSODL, while the idler photon serves as a reference. (b) Energy-time entanglement is characterized using an unbalanced interferometer with a piezo controlled phase ∆φ. (c) Polarization correlations are measured using polarization analyzers for the signal and idler photons. PBS: polarization beam splitter; BS: beam splitter; PP- KTP: periodically poled potassium titanyl phosphate...

Fig. 2. Time-bin-resolved coincidence measurements demonstrating the preservation of energy-time entanglement after propaga- tion through the FSODL. (a) Coincidence histogram of the photons before the delay line under constructive interference, centered at zero time delay. The inset shows the corresponding histogram measured under destructive interference. (b) Coincidence histogram of the delayed photons after propagation through the delay line under constructive interference, centered at 647 ns. The shaded regions indicate the post-selection window used to extract coincidence counts for interference analysis. (c) Normalized coincidence counts as a function of the two-photon phase ∆φ for...

Fig. 3. Polarization entanglement characterization after prop- agation through the optical delay line. (a) Polarization corre- lation fringes measured in the H/V and D/A bases, showing high visibility sinusoidal oscillations. (b) Real part of the re- constructed two-photon polarization density matrix for pho- tons after the delay line. The dashed boxes indicate the struc- ture of a maximally entangled polarization state. (c) Imaginary part of the reconstructed density matrix after the delay line. The dominant diagonal elements and strong coherence terms between |HH⟩and |VV⟩are consistent with the preservation of a high fidelity polarization entangled state.
